meta_description: PatchPlane is an AI change control plane for coordinating requests, runs, reviews, and merge decisions.
footer_description: Coordinate requests, runtime events, reviews, and merge decisions in one operational thread.
landing_title: One control plane for every prompt request.
landing_lede: PatchPlane gives teams one operational surface for coordinated AI change work, from intake through execution and review, across the browser today and a desktop shell later.
landing_facts_single_thread_detail: Requests, runs, reviews, and decisions stay connected.
landing_facts_operator_readable_detail: Runtime events become one timeline instead of scattered logs.
landing_signal_intro_body: One request record expands into runtime activity, review, and merge readiness without losing lineage.
landing_signal_1_detail: repo, prompt, policy, and ownership attached before execution
landing_signal_2_detail: tool calls normalized into one event feed for every operator
landing_signal_3_detail: pass/fail signals and decision lineage stay on the same thread
landing_signal_footer: Requests, runs, reviews, and decisions share one view.
landing_workflow_title: One page should explain the operating model in seconds.
landing_workflow_intro: PatchPlane is strongest when the path from request to decision feels obvious. The landing page should show that path, not the scaffolding under it.
landing_workflow_1_title: Capture the change once, with scope and policy intact.
landing_workflow_1_summary: Every run, review, and follow-up decision stays attached to the original request instead of leaking across chats, tabs, and CI logs.
landing_workflow_2_title: Project tool calls and runtime events into one operational thread.
landing_workflow_2_summary: Normalize agent events into a timeline operators can scan without caring which runtime or sandbox produced them.
landing_workflow_3_title: Hold review output in the same surface before merge moves forward.
landing_workflow_3_summary: Typed review signals stay visible next to the run itself, so merge and rollback decisions do not depend on memory.
landing_proof_title: Core capabilities should read like product mechanics, not filler.
landing_proof_intro: These are the pieces worth proving early because they define whether PatchPlane becomes a usable control plane or just another thin shell.
landing_proof_1_summary: Track prompts, scopes, policies, and the current lifecycle state.
landing_proof_2_summary: Project runtime-specific events into one shared operational timeline.
landing_proof_3_summary: Capture typed review outputs before merge and rollback logic exists.
landing_proof_4_summary: Explain how a request became a run, review, and eventual decision.
landing_platform_title: Build one shared control surface before the shells multiply.
landing_platform_intro: The browser route at /app and the future desktop shell should share the same product language, event model, and review flow. The shell can change later. The operational surface should not.
landing_cta_title: Open the product shell, then inspect the architecture notes.
landing_cta_body: The shell should feel operational. The notes should explain why the workspace stays small until the first real execution loop works.
about_hero_title: Keep the workspace small until the execution loop is proven.
about_hero_lede: PatchPlane should earn its package boundaries by proving one real flow: request intake, runtime activity, review output, and a visible decision trail.
about_principles_title: Architecture notes belong here, not on the landing page.
about_principles_intro: These principles explain why the current workspace stays compact and where future extraction pressure should come from.
about_principle_1_detail: One TanStack Start app owns both the landing page and the browser product shell so the product language stays consistent while the loop is still forming.
about_principle_2_detail: `packages/backend` keeps Convex, runtime contracts, and sandbox boundaries together until the first execution path is real enough to justify extraction.
about_principle_3_detail: Runtime adapters, policy packages, and desktop-specific bridges should split only after request coordination, runtime normalization, and review orchestration are exercised end to end.
about_package_map_title: Each package has one clear job today.
about_package_map_intro: That keeps the codebase small enough to move quickly without hiding runtime decisions in too many layers.
about_package_1_role: Landing page, browser product shell, and shared visual language.
about_package_2_role: Convex backend root plus runtime, sandbox, and policy boundaries.
about_package_3_role: Shared types, schemas, workflow statuses, and capability language.
about_bootstrap_title: Local commands and near-term implementation work stay operational.
about_bootstrap_intro: This is the place for setup notes, current constraints, and the next slice of work that should turn the shell into a real control plane.
about_next_slice_body: Wire one request mutation, one fake run action, and one event feed from Convex before introducing more runtimes, shells, or packaging layers.
app_hero_intro: This route is the working surface for coordinated change requests. Keep it operational, thin, and ready to survive both browser and desktop shells.
app_auth_intro: Convex auth state now gates the operational surface, and the browser can use authenticated queries once the backend validates the WorkOS token.
app_sign_in_prompt: Sign in to access authenticated Convex data.
app_auth_loading: Checking Convex authentication and exchanging the WorkOS token.
app_unauthenticated: The command center stays readable while signed out, but protected data stays behind Convex auth.
app_status_queued_detail: Request is recorded, scoped, and waiting for execution.
app_status_running_detail: Tool calls and artifacts are arriving in the shared event feed.
app_status_reviewed_detail: Review output is attached and ready to inform the decision.
app_workflow_status_detail: Each status belongs in the shared domain package and should receive Convex-backed transitions before new product chrome is added.
app_focus_intro: Wire one request mutation, one fake run action, and one event feed before introducing heavier integrations. Product credibility comes from a readable loop, not from more shells.
app_panel_1_detail: Prompt intake, repo scope, and policy context should all resolve into one request record before execution begins.
app_panel_2_detail: Normalized events should make tool calls, artifacts, and failures readable without opening runtime-specific consoles.
app_panel_3_detail: Review output should sit beside the run so the operator can make merge or rollback calls from the same surface.
app_authenticated_welcome: Welcome {name}. Convex validated your WorkOS token before loading this data.
